patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I keep getting out of breath doing simple things, and my feet are getting puffy.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you get winded climbing a short flight of stairs or carrying groceries; the tops of your feet look puffy and shoes feel tight by day’s end; you’re more tired with low stamina. Do not mention unless specifically asked: bluish lips/fingertips when very breathless (worse in cold), cold/color-changing fingers, exertional chest pressure that eases with rest, peeing less daytime/more at night, and slight weight gain. Voice misconceptions: “Maybe I’m just out of shape or anxious,” “swelling is from standing/salt,” “chest discomfort could be reflux.” If clinician is empathetic, you calm slightly, thank them, and admit you’re scared. nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as cryptogenic organizing pneumonia and distinguishes it from typical bacterial pneumonia (e.g., subacute course, nonresolving infiltrates, inflammatory process).
Explains that steroids are the mainstay of treatment for COP and provides a clear rationale (reduces lung inflammation/organizing process).
Explains the treatment plan using the terms prednisone and taper, including that the dose will be reduced gradually over time and why tapering is needed.
Performs an allergy_check before prescribing and does not prescribe prednisone (or any adjunct medication) if a relevant allergy is identified without addressing it.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing (e.g., uncontrolled infection suspicion, severe uncontrolled diabetes, active GI bleed, severe psychiatric history, uncontrolled hypertension) and mitigates/adjusts plan if present.

Performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing and addresses clinically meaningful interactions when identified (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers, anticoagulants/warfarin monitoring, NSAID/GI risk), though failure to do so is non-critical.

Counsels on key steroid adverse effects and precautions appropriate to context (hyperglycemia, mood changes/insomnia, GI upset, infection risk) and provides safety-net instructions for worsening dyspnea, fever, or new symptoms.

Avoids unsafe or non-guideline-concordant therapy (e.g., initiating immunosuppression without acknowledging need to exclude active infection when clinically suspected; recommending abrupt steroid stop).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose COP and start oral prednisone with a taper after required safety checks.
